content_markdown:
Two power supplies, one of constant emf $24\text{ V}$ and the other of variable emf $P$, are connected to two resistors as shown. Both power supplies have negligible internal resistances.

What is the magnitude of $P$ for the reading on the ammeter to be zero?

diagram_metadata.description: A circuit diagram containing a 24V battery in parallel with a series combination of a 9 ohm and a 3 ohm resistor. A second branch containing an ammeter and a variable power supply P is connected across the 3 ohm resistor.

vibe_explanation: For the ammeter reading to be zero, the potential difference across the branch containing the ammeter and power supply $P$ must be zero. This means the emf $P$ must exactly equal the potential drop across the $3\,\Omega$ resistor. Using the potential divider rule for the left loop: $V_{3\Omega} = 24\text{ V} \times \frac{3\,\Omega}{9\,\Omega + 3\,\Omega} = 24 \times \frac{3}{12} = 6\text{ V}$. Therefore, $P$ must be $6\text{ V}$ to oppose this potential difference perfectly.
